articles.contents:
Contents
CLAUDE.md
Project Overview
Build Commands
Architecture
Adding New Content


CLAUDE.md
This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
Project Overview
This is the course website for EDS 231: Text and Sentiment Analysis for Environmental Problems, a MEDS (Masters of Environmental Data Science) course at UC Santa Barbara‚Äôs Bren School. It is built with Distill for R Markdown.
Build Commands
All building is done through R/RStudio. There is no Makefile or CLI build script.
Render the full site (from R console):
rmarkdown::render_site()
Render a single page:
rmarkdown::render("week-2.Rmd")
Output goes to docs/ (configured in _site.yml as output_dir: docs). The site is served via GitHub Pages from that directory.
Architecture
Framework: distill::distill_website ‚Äî each .Rmd in the root renders to a page in docs/
Navigation: defined in _site.yml (navbar links, site name, GitHub link)
Theme: theme.css ‚Äî UCSB branding (navy #003660 titles, teal headers, Sanchez/Nunito Sans/Roboto Mono fonts)
Footer: _footer.html ‚Äî Bren School logo and attribution
Content structure:
- index.Rmd ‚Äî home/course overview page
- week-N.Rmd ‚Äî weekly lecture/content pages (use week-template.Rmd as a starting point)
- Labs/Lab{N}.Rmd ‚Äî student-facing lab assignments
- Labs/key-Lab{N}.Rmd ‚Äî answer keys (committed; Keys/ directory is gitignored for draft keys)
- data/ ‚Äî sample datasets (e.g., NYT article CSVs)
- Nexis/ ‚Äî Nexis Uni raw data files
Key R packages used in labs:
- tidytext ‚Äî core text analysis (tokenization, tf-idf, sentiment)
- tidyverse / ggplot2 ‚Äî wrangling and visualization
- jsonlite ‚Äî NYT API responses
- LexisNexisTools ‚Äî Nexis Uni data ingestion
Adding New Content
New weekly pages: copy week-template.Rmd, name it week-N.Rmd, then add it to the navbar in _site.yml
The Keys/ directory is gitignored ‚Äî place draft answer keys there to keep them out of version control
API keys (e.g., NYT API) should never be committed; students set them locally via Sys.setenv()

articles.title: EDS 231: Text and Sentiment Analysis for Environmental Problems
articles.description: Masters of Environmental Data Science Program, UC Santa Barbara
articles.contents:
Contents
Course description
Instructor
Important links
Weekly course schedule
Learning objectives
Course requirements
Computing
Textbook

Topics




Figure 1: Text and sentiment analysis for environmental problems



Course description
This course will cover foundations and applications of natural language processing. Problem sets and class projects will leverage common and emerging text-based data sources relevant to environmental problems, including policy documents, public comments, scientific literature, and social media. Methods covered include text processing, sentiment analysis, text classification and topic modeling.
Instructor
Mateo Robbins (mjrobbins@ucsb.edu)
Office: Bren Hall 2027
Office hours: Tuesdays, by appointment
Important links
Link to full course syllabus
Weekly course schedule
Class meets: Bren 1510
Tuesdays 12:30pm -1:45pm¬Ý ¬Ý¬Ý
Learning objectives
The goal of EDS 231 (Text and Sentiment Analysis for Environmental Problems) is to expose students to a range of text and sentiment analysis data sources, techniques and tools that can be applied to environmental problems. During this course, students will:
Become familiar with the common software packages used in text-as-data applications¬Ý
Conduct and explain each step in the text data collection, analysis, and presentation pipeline¬Ý
Evaluate examples of text analysis in the environmental science literature
Course requirements
Computing
Minimum MEDS device requirements
Up-to-date R and RStudio
Textbook
Readings will be drawn from free online ebooks:
Text Mining with R (TMR)
Supervised Machine Learning for Text Analysis in R (SMLTAR)

Topics
Week
Topic
1
Course Intro
2
Text Preprocessing and Cleaning
3
Exploratory Text Analysis and Visualization
4
Lexicon-Based Sentiment Analysis
5
Text Representation I
6
Text Representation II
7
Sentiment Analysis II
8
Classification
9
TBD
10
Group presentations

Resources
EDS 231 ‚Äî Text & Sentiment Analysis for
Environmental Problems





Environmental Data Sources

Used in This Course
Source
Description
Access
Used In
New York Times API
Full-text article search across the NYT archive. Returns metadata,
snippets, and article URLs via JSON. Free with registration;
rate-limited.
API key via NYT Developer Portal
Lab 1
Nexis Uni
Licensed news and publication database with full-text exports in
.docx format. Covers thousands of outlets globally.
UCSB Library login
Labs 2 & 4
Bluesky / AT Protocol
Decentralized social media platform. The {atrrr}
package provides a tidy R interface for searching posts by keyword,
hashtag, or user.
Free account + app password
Lab 3



Key Readings
Studies applying text and sentiment analysis to environmental
research questions. Organized thematically.

Foundational NLP & Sentiment Methods
Show readings

Citation


Year


Key Contribution


Silge & Robinson ‚Äî
Text Mining with R (O‚ÄôReilly)


2017


Canonical tidy text analysis reference; covers sentiment, tf-idf, topic
models in R


Hvitfeldt & Silge ‚Äî Supervised ML
for Text Analysis in R


2022


ML-based text classification, embeddings, and modeling workflows in R


Blei, Ng &
Jordan ‚Äî JMLR


2003


Original LDA paper; the generative model underlying most topic modeling
workflows


Mikolov et al.¬Ý‚Äî
arXiv


2013


Word2Vec; introduced dense word embeddings that underpin modern NLP



Climate & Weather
Show readings

Citation


Year


Key Contribution


L√ºdecke et
al.¬Ý‚Äî Nature Climate Change


2021


Sentiment analysis of climate change Twitter discourse across countries;
identifies negativity bias and polarization


Cody et al.¬Ý‚Äî
Environmental Research Letters


2015


Sentiment in climate-related tweets tracks real-world climate events;
validates social media as a climate signal


Tvinnereim et
al.¬Ý‚Äî Global Environmental Change


2020


Open-ended survey responses on climate change coded with topic models;
reveals public framing diverges from expert framing



Biodiversity & Conservation
Show readings

Citation


Year


Key Contribution


Westgate
et al.¬Ý‚Äî Conservation Biology


2015


Topic modeling of conservation literature to identify research gaps and
emerging themes


Nolan et
al.¬Ý‚Äî Methods in Ecology & Evolution


2021


pyResearchInsights: automated topic modeling pipeline for ecology and
conservation abstracts


Valle et
al.¬Ý‚Äî Ecology Letters


2014


LDA applied to species assemblage data; bridges ecological community
analysis and NLP methods


Edelmann et
al.¬Ý‚Äî Conservation Biology


2025


Review of LLMs and NLP for evidence synthesis in conservation social
science



Pollution & Environmental Health
Show readings

Citation


Year


Key Contribution


Feng et
al.¬Ý‚Äî PLOS ONE


2015


LDA on Weibo posts to track public awareness of PM2.5 air quality in
China


Wang
& Jia ‚Äî Journal of Cleaner Production


2021


Social media discourse on air pollution linked to bottom-up
environmental governance pressure


Chang
et al.¬Ý‚Äî Journal of Environmental Management


2023


Twitter-based civil complaints about urban pollution spatially matched
to monitoring data in Taipei


Lin et al.¬Ý‚Äî
IJERPH


2021


LDA + PLS-SEM on social media mining to model air pollution adaptation
behavior



Corporate ESG & Environmental Reporting
Show readings

Citation


Year


Key Contribution


Sz√©kely
& vom Brocke ‚Äî PLOS ONE


2017


LDA on GRI sustainability reports to track longitudinal shifts in
corporate environmental framing


Kriebel
& Foege ‚Äî Decision Support Systems


2024


Benchmarks NLP methods (LDA, BERT, ChatGPT) for sustainability
disclosure analysis in 10-K filings


Gorovaia et al.¬Ý‚Äî
Sustainability


2025


LDA + TF-IDF pipeline for greenwashing detection using a Greenwashing
Severity Index




R Packages for Text Analysis

Core Text Analysis
Package
Purpose
Install
tidytext
Tidy-format text mining: tokenization, tf-idf, sentiment joins,
topic model tidying
install.packages("tidytext")
quanteda
High-performance corpus and document-feature matrix (DFM)
construction
install.packages("quanteda")
quanteda.textstats
Keyness, readability, lexical diversity statistics on DFMs
install.packages("quanteda.textstats")
quanteda.textplots
Wordclouds, keyness plots, and other quanteda visualizations
install.packages("quanteda.textplots")
stringr
Consistent string manipulation functions (part of
{tidyverse})
install.packages("stringr")


Preprocessing
Package
Purpose
Install
SnowballC
Porter stemmer for 15+ languages
install.packages("SnowballC")
textstem
Lemmatization using the Hunspell dictionary
install.packages("textstem")


Sentiment Analysis
Package
Purpose
Install
sentimentr
Sentence-level sentiment with valence shifters (negation,
amplifiers, de-amplifiers)
install.packages("sentimentr")
tidyvader
Tidy interface to VADER ‚Äî optimized for social media, handles slang
and punctuation
remotes::install_github("chris31415926535/tidyvader")
vader
Direct VADER implementation; returns compound, positive, negative,
and neutral scores
install.packages("vader")


Topic Modeling
Package
Purpose
Install
topicmodels
LDA and CTM topic models; interfaces with {tidytext}
via tidy()
install.packages("topicmodels")
ldatuning
Metrics (CaoJuan2009, Deveaud2014, etc.) for selecting the number of
topics k
install.packages("ldatuning")
LDAvis
Interactive visualization of topic-word distributions and intertopic
distances
install.packages("LDAvis")
stm
Structural Topic Model ‚Äî allows topic prevalence and content to vary
with document covariates
install.packages("stm")


Data Access
Package
Purpose
Install
jsonlite
Parse JSON responses from APIs (NYT, etc.) into R data frames
install.packages("jsonlite")
LexisNexisTools
Parse Nexis Uni .docx exports into tidy data
frames
install.packages("LexisNexisTools")
atrrr
Bluesky (AT Protocol) API client ‚Äî search posts, retrieve feeds,
authenticate
install.packages("atrrr")











  This website was made with distill by RStudio.

Week 2: NYT API
EDS 231 ‚Äî Spring 2026
April 14, 2026





An introduction to text preprocessing and the New York Times article
API.


Learning Objectives
By the end of this week, you will be able to:
Make API calls in R ‚Äî constructing URLs with query parameters and
authenticating with an API key
Conduct exploratory data analysis ‚Äî visualizing temporal patterns in
media coverage using ggplot2
Apply string manipulation ‚Äî cleaning strings using functions like
str* and gsub()
Use stop word dictionaries ‚Äî removing common uninformative words
from text data to prepare it for analysis


Recommended Reading
üìñ TMR
1.0-1.3, SMLTR

Week 3: Nexis Uni
EDS 231 ‚Äî Spring 2026
April 7, 2026





Nexis Uni data.


Learning Objectives
By the end of this week, you will be able to: ‚Äì>
Clean raw corpus text ‚Äî removing URLs, encoding artifacts, and
source-specific boilerplate
Build domain-specific stopword lists ‚Äî identifying and removing
corpus-specific uninformative terms beyond standard lexicons
Apply stemming and lemmatization ‚Äî reducing tokens to base forms
using the Porter stemmer and dictionary-based lemmatization, and
evaluate the tradeoffs between them
Conduct frequency analysis ‚Äî counting and visualizing term frequency
across the full corpus and by source
Perform keyness analysis ‚Äî comparing word use across sub-corpora
using log-likelihood (G¬≤) with quanteda to identify characteristic
terms


Recommended Reading
üìñ SMLTR Ch.4


Lecture Materials

(parts 7. and 8.). Submit your .Rmd and knitted document (HTML or PDF)
to Canvas.

Week 4: Lexicon-Based Sentiment
Analysis
EDS 231 ‚Äî Spring 2026
April 14, 2026





Lexicon-based sentiment analysis applied to Bluesky social media
data.


Learning Objectives
By the end of this week, you will be able to:
Authenticate with a social media API and retrieve posts by keyword
using {atrrr}
Clean and tokenize social media text for downstream sentiment
analysis
Apply and interpret multiple lexicon-based sentiment methods
Identify common failure modes of general-purpose lexicons on
domain-specific environmental text
Compare method outputs using correlation matrices and distribution
visualizations
Evaluate which method is most appropriate for a given corpus and
research question


Recommended Reading
üìñ SMLTR Ch. 2 ‚Äî Sentiment
Analysis

with R ‚Äî Ch. 2: Sentiment Analysis with Tidy Data

Complete the assignment laid out in the Lab 3 markdown. Submit your

stored in .Renviron and do not appear anywhere in your
submitted file. Look your knitted product over before submission to make
sure all plots rendered and extraneous print-outs did not render.

Week 5: Topic Modeling Lab Preparation
EDS 231 ‚Äî Spring 2026
April 21, 2026





Selection of articles to serve as data for next week‚Äôs topic modeling
lab.


Learning Objectives
By the end of NEXT week, you will be able to:
Explain the generative assumptions behind Latent Dirichlet
Allocation (LDA)
Construct a document-feature matrix (DFM) suitable for topic
modeling using {quanteda}
Select a value of k using theory, optimization metrics
(ldatuning), and interpretability
Interpret the beta (word-topic) and theta (topic-document) posterior
distributions
Visualize topic structure with term plots and
{LDAvis}
Apply topic modeling to a real news corpus and characterize emergent
themes


Recommended Reading
üìñ Text Mining
with R ‚Äî Ch. 6: Topic Modeling
üìñ Blei, Ng &
Jordan (2003) ‚Äî Latent Dirichlet Allocation, JMLR


Lecture Materials

Slides
üñ• Slide deck from lecture
View
Slides


Code & Data
üíª R scripts used during class:




Preparing for Lab 4: Getting Your Data from Nexis Uni
Lab 4 will ask you to run a topic model on a corpus of news articles
you retrieve yourself from Nexis Uni. Follow the steps
below before lab so you arrive with data ready to go.

Step 1 ‚Äî Search Nexis Uni
Go to the UCSB Library Nexis
Uni portal and search for Nexis Uni.
Run a keyword search on a topic of your choosing.
Filter to News sources. You can use any other
filters you deem appropriate for your purposes. Aim for around
100 articles** ‚Äî enough for meaningful topics but not
so many that model fitting is slow.
Select download format ofWord (.docx). You‚Äôll have
to select articles in batches for download.


Step 2 ‚Äî Organize your files
Place all downloaded .docx files in a folder inside your
project:
your-project/
  Nexis/
    your-topic/
      export1.docx
      export2.docx


Step 3 ‚Äî Read the data into R
The {LexisNexisTools} package parses the Nexis
.docx exports. Install it if you haven‚Äôt already:
install.packages("LexisNexisTools")
Then use the code below to load your files and assemble a tidy data
frame. The commented-out block below is the standard workflow ‚Äî update
the path argument to match your folder name:
library(LexisNexisTools)
library(tidyverse)

pre_files <- list.files(
  path = here::here("Nexis", "your-topic"),   # update to your folder
  pattern = "\\.docx$",
  full.names = TRUE,
  recursive = TRUE,
  ignore.case = TRUE
)

pre_dat <- lnt_read(pre_files)

pre_meta_df       <- pre_dat@meta
pre_articles_df   <- pre_dat@articles
pre_paragraphs_df <- pre_dat@paragraphs

df <- tibble(
  date     = pre_meta_df$Date,
  headline = pre_meta_df$Headline,
  id       = pre_meta_df$ID,
  text     = pre_articles_df$Article,
  source   = pre_meta_df$Newspaper
)



üìÇ Assignment not due next week

Lab 4 ‚Äî Topic Modeling Graded ¬∑ 11
pts



Due: May 12 @ 11:59 pm

Week XX: Week Title
EDS 231 ‚Äî Spring 2026





Getting oriented: what we‚Äôll cover, how we‚Äôll work, and why it
